old_claim.note: Quantum Physics provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from the broad semiconductors node to quantum_physics; quantum theory explains and improves later semiconductor engineering but postdates the 1874 rectifier effect.

source_shape.source_locator: Milestone text describing Braun's 1874 observation of current flowing one way at a metal-galena contact.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The CHM milestone describes the semiconductor point-contact rectifier effect as an 1874 experimental observation, before quantum theory as dated in the graph.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes a prerequisite edge because the broad node now covers the observed semiconductor effect, while quantum physics belongs as explanatory and enabling context for later semiconductor-device engineering.

ontology_before: The broad Semiconductors node treated quantum physics as upstream of all semiconductor behavior, which made a 1900 scientific theory a prerequisite for an 1874 observed device effect.
ontology_after: The Semiconductors node can predate quantum theory; quantum physics remains directly connected to later nodes where device theory or design depends on it.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the graph no longer makes a later explanatory theory a prerequisite for an earlier observed semiconductor effect. Preserved: modern semiconductor devices can still cite quantum physics independently.
would_reject_if: The semiconductors node were narrowed to mean quantum-theory-based semiconductor device engineering rather than materials and early rectifiers.
A source showed that Braun's 1874 semiconductor rectifier observation depended on quantum physics as an available theory or method.
Removing the edge also removed quantum-physics context from transistor, diode, photovoltaic, or other later semiconductor-device nodes.
short_reason: Quantum physics is explanatory and enabling for later semiconductor engineering, but it is not a prerequisite for the 1874 semiconductor rectifier effect.
